title: Podman fails to refresh devices passed in the container
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Attach device

2. Run podman with `--device <dir>`

3. Detach device

**Describe the results you received:**

The container will continue to show the devices that existed during startup. It does not add new or remove old devices.

**Describe the results you expected:**

The container should see the changes happening on the host.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

The following shows a container which had the device attached during startup. But now the device is removed. Still it is visible in the filesystem of the container. Exiting and re-starting the container does a refresh.

~~~
root@bf6d57f194de:/# find /dev/snd
/dev/snd
/dev/snd/timer
/dev/snd/seq
/dev/snd/pcmC1D0p
/dev/snd/pcmC1D0c
/dev/snd/pcmC0D1p
/dev/snd/pcmC0D0p
/dev/snd/controlC1
/dev/snd/controlC0
root@bf6d57f194de:/# exit
[root@localhost ~]# podman run --rm -ti --device /dev/snd --entrypoint bash  ghcr.io/drogue-iot/hey-rodney-pocketsphinx:latest
root@ff86315988d4:/# find /dev/snd
/dev/snd
/dev/snd/timer
/dev/snd/seq
/dev/snd/pcmC0D1p
/dev/snd/pcmC0D0p
/dev/snd/controlC0

~~~

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.1.1
API Version:  2.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.2
Built:        Sun Oct  4 23:25:48 2020
OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
host:
  arch: arm64
  buildahVersion: 1.16.1
  cgroupManager: systemd
  cgroupVersion: v2
  conmon:
    package: conmon-2.0.21-3.fc33.aarch64
    path: /usr/bin/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 2.0.21, commit: 66d50d2f6c81e28bacd99ee8e8d222f028eef63f'
  cpus: 4
  distribution:
    distribution: fedora
    version: "33"
  eventLogger: journald
  hostname: localhost.localdomain
  idMappings:
    gidmap: null
    uidmap: null
  kernel: 5.8.15-301.fc33.aarch64
  linkmode: dynamic
  memFree: 104620032
  memTotal: 980905984
  ociRuntime:
    name: crun
    package: crun-0.15-5.fc33.aarch64
    path: /usr/bin/crun
    version: |-
      crun version 0.15
      commit: 56ca95e61639510c7dbd39ff512f80f626404969
      spec: 1.0.0
      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +YAJL
  os: linux
  remoteSocket:
    path: /run/podman/podman.sock
  rootless: false
  slirp4netns:
    executable: ""
    package: ""
    version: ""
  swapFree: 0
  swapTotal: 0
  uptime: 7h 14m 12.76s (Approximately 0.29 days)
registries:
  search:
  - registry.fedoraproject.org
  - registry.access.redhat.com
  - registry.centos.org
  - docker.io
store:
  configFile: /etc/containers/storage.conf
  containerStore:
    number: 0
    paused: 0
    running: 0
    stopped: 0
  graphDriverName: overlay
  graphOptions:
    overlay.mountopt: nodev,metacopy=on
  graphRoot: /var/lib/containers/storage
  graphStatus:
    Backing Filesystem: extfs
    Native Overlay Diff: "false"
    Supports d_type: "true"
    Using metacopy: "true"
  imageStore:
    number: 5
  runRoot: /var/run/containers/storage
  volumePath: /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes
version:
  APIVersion: 2.0.0
  Built: 1601853948
  BuiltTime: Sun Oct  4 23:25:48 2020
  GitCommit: ""
  GoVersion: go1.15.2
  OsArch: linux/arm64

```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

Fedora IoT: there is no `rpm` or `apt`.

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

No

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Same behavior on Fedora IoT as well as Fedora 33.

comments.body:
Podman is not watching the device directory to see changes, it really can not, since it goes away in the --detached mode.
Theoretically we could get conmon to watch the directory, and add/remove devices, but this would only work in rootfull mode.
if you mount the /dev/dir into the container then it should work, although SELinux might become MAD, and block some access.

I don't think this is expected behavior. If you explicitly tell Podman to mount, say, `/dev/sdb` into a container, and `/dev/sdb` suddenly becomes inaccessible, Podman can no longer do what you asked (mount the given device in) and should error.

If you want to monitor `/dev` for changes, mounting `/dev/` into the container seems appropriate.

My use case here is device hot-plugging, adding audio devices (or loosing them) during runtime of a container.

> If you want to monitor /dev for changes, mounting /dev/ into the container seems appropriate.

From what I read in the comment above, that would cause problems with SElinux?! And thus is not a viable solution.

IIRC there is a `:z` flag for `--volume`, would that do the trick?
No, but the SELinux Udica team is working on a solution. @wrabcak PTAL
@rhatdan Did I get that right: the solution would be that in a future version `--volume` plus `:z` would be sufficient to achieve this? That would be fine.

Hello @ctron, for your usecase this is not the right approach. Because also another processes and services can access devices you cannot change the SELinux label of the device (using :z will do that for you). For this purpose, you need to use custom SELinux policy for your container so the container can access` /dev ` and device you want to mount into container. Recently, I added new feature to the udica so you can generate the policy for your containers. Please look on the project [container/udica](https://github.com/containers/udica) . 

Your ideal steps should be: 
```
# dnf install udica -y
# podman inspect <name_of_the_container> | udica my_device_container_name
```

And follow the output from udica tool. 

If you have any question, please let me know.
Thanks @wrabcak for the help. I will try that out, but probably not this year anymore :grinning:
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Not sure there is much podman can do with this, so closing.
